feedback.text: I welcome the Commissions plan for a Digital Fairness Act. The fitness check found that consumers face dark patterns, addictive design and unfair personalisation. Tackling these practices is vital, but fair digital markets also require that software we buy remains usable. Many video games are sold as onlineonly products. When publishers shut down the servers that make them work, the game disappears even though players paid for it. Ubisofts closure of its racing game The Crew in March 2024 sparked the Stop Killing Games campaign and a citizens initiative. The problem is broader than one title: more and more games are designed so that they become useless when support ends. This undermines trust and leaves consumers with nothing for their money. At present, most games are licensed rather than sold. EndUser Licence Agreements often reserve the right to end access at any time, yet EU law says a term that lets a supplier end an indeterminate contract without reasonable notice is unfair. Directive 2019/770 recognises digital games as digital content and calls for clear rules. When people buy a game they expect to play it; a licence that can be revoked arbitrarily deprives them of the product they paid for. Games also have artistic and cultural value, as a 2023 report for the Commission noted, so their disappearance is a loss to culture as well as to individual consumers. The Digital Fairness Act should address this gap. My suggestions are: Treat video games and other software sold to consumers as products with a reasonable expectation of durability, not as licences that can be withdrawn at will. Digital content is already recognised in EU law; consumer rights should reflect this. Prohibit contract terms that allow publishers to terminate access without reasonable notice. Directive 93/13/EEC already classes such terms as unfair. The Act should require clear prior notice before shutting down a game and should prohibit companies from removing access overnight. Require publishers who discontinue an online service, where technically possible, to provide an update that preserves offline play or to offer partial refunds. The Stop Killing Games initiative does not demand indefinite support; it asks only for postendofservice local accessibility. This would balance developers rights with consumers rights. Promote game preservation. The Commissions own research emphasises that video games are part of our cultural heritage. Designing games so they can be archived or emulated after their commercial life would serve cultural diversity and sustainability. Apply these rules to existing titles. Transitional measures could require publishers of currently sold games to guarantee a minimum lifespan or make plans for local access before closing servers. Europe has long been a leader in protecting consumers. The Digital Fairness Act offers a historic opportunity to extend that protection to digital entertainment. Ensuring that games are treated as products, with durability, fair contract terms and preservation, will strengthen trust, support creative industries and safeguard cultural heritage. I urge the Commission to include explicit rules preventing the disabling of purchased games and to enshrine the simple principle that a product sold should remain a product.
